text: This is the case of a laparoscopic splenectomy performed for a massive spleen in a young man who had a condition called hereditary spherocytosis. He'd had a cholecystectomy as a child and his spleen was enlarging and becoming painful.
The surgeon utilizes a five-port laparoscopic technique, having added an additional port to facilitate the dissection of the enlarged spleen.

The patient is positioned on his right side, using optical entry for laparoscopic access. The spleen is visibly displaced to the left side, creating an unusual and limited working space for the procedure.

The surgeon inserts a twelve millimeter port for the camera and an additional twelve millimeter port, along with two five millimeter ports, facilitating the laparoscopic dissection of tissues surrounding the spleen.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon by taking down the tissues holding the splenic flexure against the spleen, creating separation between the colon and the spleen.
The surgeon uses an angled scope to perform the laparoscopic splenectomy, providing a two-dimensional view of the surgical field.
A five millimeter port is inserted in the flank to aid in retracting the spleen and facilitate the passage of instruments at various angles within the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the very large spleen from its inferior edge, revealing some of the early attachments around the hilum of the spleen.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the tissue of the lino-renal ligament, which connects the spleen to the kidney, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon successfully lifts the inferior pole of the large spleen, which had been difficult to manipulate due to its size and tendency to obstruct the view.
An additional port is inserted to facilitate dissection, allowing the surgeon to pass an instrument through it to divide the fatty tissue connected to the inferior pole of the spleen.

The surgeon uses the N-seal bipolar instrument to divide and ligate small tributaries of the spleen, sealing the vessels with its dual-sided burning blade. This process also aids in mobilizing fat away from the inferior pole of the spleen.
The surgeon exposes the tissue between the spleen and the stomach, revealing the hilum of the spleen where the blood vessels enter.

The ENSEAL device is used to open the fat between the spleen and the stomach, exposing the lesser sac behind the stomach. The surgeon carefully divides the short gastric vessels running between the closely applied stomach and spleen, ensuring no damage to the stomach or bleeding occurs.
The surgeon uses the N-seal device to securely divide the posterior short gastric vessels, which are visible near the spleen. The vessels separate with relative ease during the procedure.
The surgeon divides extra tissue near the upper pole of the spleen, further freeing the stomach from the spleen and exposing the hilum of the spleen.
The surgeon dissects and divides the peritoneum over the splenic hilum, revealing the splenic artery. The dissection continues, opening a wide area and exposing the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses an energy device to carefully divide the fatty tissue away from the pancreas, avoiding proximity to the pancreas itself. The capsule of the hilum of the spleen is divided, along with the peritoneum surrounding the large blood vessels entering the spleen.
The surgeon prepares to control the splenic artery before addressing the splenic vein, aiming to decompress the spleen and reduce blood loss during its removal.

The surgeon observes the bifurcation of the splenic artery, initially unclear if the inferior branch supplies the spleen. Careful dissection reveals the early branch is a pancreatic branch, which is preserved while dissecting the vessel.

The real advantage of the laparoscope once you get everything in position is that we're really operating up under a deep cave of the abdomen here with the roof of that cave at the moment formed by the spleen itself. and this is very awkward to do as open surgery does require a very large incision to do this as open surgery and therefore where possible we attempt splenectomy with a laparoscopic approach. There's no doubt though that a spleen of this size is challenging to perform safely laparoscopically.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior aspect of the splenic vein, ensuring to stay close to the vessel to avoid damaging the tail of the pancreas, which could potentially wrap around and cause complications such as a pancreatic leak.

The surgeon carefully cleans fatty tissue on the opposite side of the splenic artery, gradually mobilizing the vessel running into the spleen. A stepwise approach is used to safely perform the dissection, freeing up one area at a time to facilitate subsequent steps.

The surgeon carefully separates the splenic artery from the vein, avoiding direct manipulation to prevent tearing. Surrounding nerves and lymphatics are meticulously dissected away from the artery, which is now exposed over a sufficient length for ligation. The distal artery is prepared for ligation.
The surgeon ligates the artery using metallic clips, placing an extra third clip for added security to prevent rapid bleeding. The surrounding fat wrapping around the vein is further mobilized.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic vein, ensuring no pancreatic tissue is within the surrounding fat. The spleen is visibly decompressing through the vein, while the posterior hiloperitoneum is further divided.
The surgeon carefully performs a meticulous dissection around the splenic vein, cautiously avoiding potential pancreatic tissue that may be present, despite its unlikely appearance on the scan.

The surgeon isolates the vein, ensuring it is separated from any potential pancreatic tissue. Clips are removed from the spleen side of the artery using an end seal device to prevent misfires when applying the stapler.

A vascular stapler is introduced into the surgical field. The camera view shifts slightly as it navigates the tight space with other instruments. The surgeon ensures the stapler is properly positioned across the vein before deploying the staples to secure the tissue.

With the hilum fully dissected, the surgeon focuses on the remaining parts of the liana renal ligament, adjusting the patient's position to facilitate the spleen's forward movement. The final attachments between the spleen and diaphragm are severed using a harmonic scalpel, freeing the spleen completely.

The surgeon ensures the spleen is completely free before introducing an Alexis extraction bag through an enlarged trocar site. The initial attempt to use a rolled-up foot bag proved challenging to manipulate around the spleen.

The surgeon enlarges a 12mm trocar site to 15mm to pass an Alexis extraction bag. The semi-stiff plastic rim of the bag is positioned flat with the posterior aspect tucked behind the inferior pole of the spleen. The anterior part of the hoop is lifted to gradually encase the spleen, continuing up to the superior pole. The camera is occasionally brushed by the bag during this process. The superior pole is finally tucked into the bag, and the ring is delivered outside the incision. A small hole in the bag allows for instruments to be inserted for spleen morselation, which is completed without perforating the bag.

we double checked that there was no bleeding laparoscopically and closed without a drain the post-operative course was uneventful apart from some surgical emphysema that developed over the left flank which i believe was from pumping air in at the time of manipulating the spleen within the bag as we were morselating it he's had an uneventful recovery ever since
